title: Reading Git Log
intro: Your teammate says the latest deploy broke the login page. The site was working fine yesterday. Just like a detective reviewing security camera footage to find when something went wrong, you need to use Git's history to figure out what changed and who changed it.

teach.title: Git Log = Your Project's Diary
teach.body: Imagine your project keeps a diary. Every time someone makes a commit, an entry is written: who did it, when, and a short note about what changed. `git log` lets you open that diary and read through every entry, from newest to oldest.

teach.title: What You'll See in the Log
teach.body:
Each entry in `git log` shows:

- **Commit hash** -- A unique ID like `a1b2c3d` (think of it as a serial number for that save point)
- **Author** -- Who made the change
- **Date** -- When it happened
- **Message** -- What they said they changed

The most recent commit appears at the top.

teach.title: Useful Log Commands
teach.code:
git log                # Full history with details
git log --oneline      # Compact view: hash + message
git show a1b2c3d       # See exactly what changed in one commit
teach.annotation: Use `git log --oneline` for a quick overview, then `git show <hash>` to dive into a specific commit.

teach.title: Git Log = Security Camera Footage
teach.body: Imagine a store gets robbed. The first thing the detective does is check the security camera footage -- who was in the store, what time did they enter, what did they do? `git log` is your project's security camera. When a bug appears, you rewind the footage to see exactly which change introduced it and who made it.

teach.title: Why This Matters for Debugging
teach.body: When something breaks, `git log` is your detective tool. You can scan recent commits to find which change likely caused the problem, then use `git show` to see exactly what lines were added or removed. It's much faster than guessing.

Debugging quickly is what turns a $30/hour coder into a $150/hour consultant. Mastering `git log` means you can find and fix bugs in minutes instead of hours -- and that speed is what clients pay premium rates for.
`git log` shows your project's complete history -- use it to find out what changed, when, and by whom when something goes wrong.

start.narrative: The login page is showing a blank white screen after today's deploy. Your teammate is panicking. What's your first move?

choices.text: Run 'git log --oneline' to see recent commits
Immediately revert to yesterday's version
Start rewriting the login page from scratch

check-log.narrative:
Smart move! You run 'git log --oneline' and see:

a1b2c3d Fix navbar styling
b4e5f6a Update login form validation
c7d8e9f Add new marketing banner
d0e1f2a Initial deploy setup

One of these commits likely caused the issue. Which one do you investigate first?

choices.text: Check 'b4e5f6a Update login form validation' -- it directly touches login
Check 'a1b2c3d Fix navbar styling' -- it's the most recent
Check all commits one by one starting from the oldest

revert-blind.narrative: You revert without checking what changed. The login page works again, but now the new marketing banner that your boss asked for is also gone. They're not happy. It would have been better to check the log first to understand what specifically broke things.

choices.text: Go back and check the log to understand what happened

rewrite.narrative: Rewriting the entire login page would take hours and you'd lose all the recent improvements. Git's history has the answer -- you just need to look at what changed recently.

choices.text: Okay, let me check git log instead

inspect-commit.narrative: You suspect 'b4e5f6a Update login form validation'. The commit message tells you what someone *intended* to do, but you need to see the actual code change. How do you read exactly what was modified?

choices.text: Run 'git show b4e5f6a' to see the full diff for that commit
Run 'git log -p b4e5f6a' so I can scroll through diffs of multiple commits
Open the file in my editor and try to guess which lines changed

found-bug.narrative: The diff is clear: the commit changed the form's action URL from '/api/login' to '/api/loign' (typo) and also added two valid validation rules. The bug is one line. How do you fix this without losing the validation work?

choices.text: Edit the typo, commit just that line, push -- keep the validation rules intact
Run 'git revert b4e5f6a' to undo the entire commit, then re-add validation later
Hard-reset main to the commit before b4e5f6a

verify-fix.narrative: The fix is in. Before you tell your teammate the fire is out, how do you confirm the deploy actually picked up your patch and the login page works for users?

choices.text: Run 'git log --oneline -3' to confirm my fix is the latest commit, then load the production login page in an incognito window
Trust the CI green check and tell the teammate it's done
Refresh production once -- if it loads, we're good

prevent-future.narrative: Login works again. Now: this typo slipped through because nobody read the diff before merging. What's the most practical safeguard against the next typo?

choices.text: Add a Playwright smoke test that POSTs to /api/login on every PR -- a 404 fails the build
Require code review on every PR touching the login form
Send a Slack reminder to be careful with API URLs

end-good.narrative: You used 'git log' to scan recent history, picked the most likely commit from its message, used 'git show' to read the actual diff, fixed only the broken line, verified the deploy in production, and added a smoke test that catches this exact class of bug on the next PR. Total downtime: under 10 minutes.
end-good.feedback: Professional incident response with Git. The pattern -- log, show, targeted fix, verify, automate the catch -- is the difference between firefighting forever and shrinking the bug surface over time. The smoke test means this bug class is now extinct in your repo.

end-okay.narrative: You found and fixed the bug cleanly. Code review will help, but reviewers miss typos in long diffs all the time -- that's how this one got in to begin with.
end-okay.feedback: Solid debugging. Review is good, but a smoke test is better: it never gets tired, never skims, never approves a PR at 5pm on Friday. Pair the review with one automated check per critical user flow and you'll stop seeing this kind of regression.

end-weak.narrative: The login works for now, but nothing has changed about how the team ships. The next typo in a commit will land just as easily, and you'll be running 'git log' again next week.
end-weak.feedback: You handled the bug, but a Slack reminder is a wish, not a safeguard. People forget. CI doesn't. Add even one smoke test for the login flow and you'll save hours of future debugging.
